content:
5 min read
3 hours ago

--

The most argued-about question in frontend auth has a real answer — it’s just not the one most blog posts give you.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

You’ve got a token. The server handed it to your React app. Now what — localStorage.setItem and move on? That one reflex is responsible for a surprising share of real-world account takeovers. Let me walk you through the actual decision, because "just use httpOnly cookies" and "just use localStorage" are both wrong as blanket advice.
First, the two threats you’re actually choosing between
Every storage decision is really a bet about which attack you’re more exposed to. There are two, and they pull in opposite directions.
XSS (Cross-Site Scripting): Malicious JavaScript runs in your page. Maybe through a compromised npm dependency, a bad third-party script, or an unescaped user input. Once attacker code runs in your origin, it can read anything your own JavaScript can read.
CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery): Another site tricks the user’s browser into making an authenticated request to your API — relying on the fact that browsers attach cookies automatically, even on cross-site requests.
Here’s the key insight: the storage that protects you from one tends to expose you to the other. That’s why there’s no single right answer divorced from context.
JS can read it? → exposed to XSS
Browser auto-sends it? → exposed to CSRFHold that frame. Now let’s run each option through it.
Option A — localStorage
The default everyone reaches for.
localStorage.setItem("token", jwt);
// later, on every request:
fetch("/api/me", {
headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${localStorage.getItem("token")}` },
});The good: Dead simple. Survives page reloads and tabs. You manually attach it as a header, so cross-site requests can’t carry it automatically — meaning it’s immune to CSRF.
The fatal flaw: Any JavaScript on your page can read it. One XSS hole — one compromised dependency in your bundle — and the attacker does localStorage.getItem("token") and walks away with a valid credential. There's nothing the browser does to stop them.
localStorage trades CSRF-safety for total XSS-exposure. For a high-value session token, that's usually a bad trade.
Option B — httpOnly cookies
The token lives in a cookie the browser sets httpOnly, Secure, and SameSite.
Set-Cookie: token=eyJ...; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict; Path=/The good: httpOnly means JavaScript literally cannot read it — document.cookie won't show it. So even if you have an XSS bug, the attacker can't exfiltrate the token to their own server. That's a meaningful upgrade.
The catch: Cookies are sent automatically by the browser, which is exactly the CSRF attack surface. You neutralize that with SameSite=Strict or Lax (so the cookie isn't sent on cross-site requests) and, for anything sensitive, a CSRF token or double-submit pattern as backup.
The nuance people miss: httpOnly does not make you XSS-proof. An attacker who can run JS in your page can still use the cookie — it rides along on every request they trigger from your origin. What they can't do is steal the token and reuse it elsewhere later. That's a real but partial win.
Option C — in-memory (a JS variable)
Keep the access token in a plain variable or a closure — never written to storage at all.
let accessToken = null;export const setToken = (t) => { accessToken = t; };
export const getToken = () => accessToken;The good: It’s the smallest possible attack surface. Nothing persisted to disk, nothing in document.cookie, nothing in localStorage to scrape. When the tab closes or reloads, it's gone.
The catch: That last part — it’s gone on reload. A user refreshing the page would be logged out, which is unacceptable UX on its own. In-memory only works as part of a pair: short-lived access token in memory, plus a way to silently get a new one after reload.
The recommendation I actually give
Put the pieces together and a clear pattern emerges — and it’s a hybrid, not a single store:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Access token → in memory (JS variable) │ short-lived, never persisted
│ Refresh token → httpOnly, Secure, SameSite │ JS can't read it, used only to refresh
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘Why this wins:

• The access token in memory means there’s nothing for XSS to scrape off disk, and you attach it manually as a header (no CSRF).

• The refresh token in an httpOnly cookie means even if the page is compromised, the attacker can’t read the long-lived credential and walk away with a durable session.

• On page reload, the in-memory access token is gone — so you make one call to your refresh endpoint, the browser sends the httpOnly refresh cookie automatically, and you get a fresh access token back into memory. The user never notices.
This “silent refresh on load” dance is the part that makes in-memory storage viable, and it’s exactly the mechanism the refresh token walkthrough in React and Node builds step by step. If you only store the access token in memory and skip the refresh flow, you’ll have great security and miserable UX.
A decision table you can actually use
WhereXSS theft riskCSRF riskSurvives reloadVerdictlocalStorageHigh (readable)NoneYesAvoid for session tokenshttpOnly cookieToken can't be readYes — needs SameSite/CSRF tokenYesGood for refresh tokenIn-memoryLowestNoneNoBest for access token (+ refresh flow)
What this means for your code review
Next time you see localStorage.setItem("token", ...) in a PR, the question isn't "does it work" — it works fine. The question is: what's our XSS posture, and are we comfortable handing the whole session to anyone who finds one script injection? For most apps, the answer pushes you toward the hybrid above.
And remember the prerequisite that makes all of this moot if you get it wrong: short access-token lifetimes. A token stolen from memory or storage is far less scary when it expires in 15 minutes. That, plus algorithm pinning and payload hygiene, is covered in the JWT best practices checklist — read it alongside this one.
Closing
There’s no universally “secure” place to put a JWT — there’s only the storage choice that matches the threats you actually face. For most production apps: access token in memory, refresh token in an httpOnly cookie, short lifetimes throughout.
Frontend Master walks through this whole tradeoff with diagrams in the full video: Where Should You Store JWT?. More frontend and security deep dives live on the channel @rahuulmiishra, and if you want to talk through your app’s specific setup, book a mock interview with Frontend Master: https://allahabadi.dev/frontend-mock-interview/.
